1: וַיהוָה פָּקַד אֶת-שָׂרָה (waYHWH pāqaḏ ʾet-śārāh) — "the LORD visited Sarah." Pāqaḏ (to visit, attend to, appoint, muster) — a verb with two contrasting senses that must be determined from context: divine visitation in blessing (Ruth 1:6; 1 Samuel 2:21; Luke 1:68: "he has visited/redeemed his people") and divine visitation in judgment (Exodus 32:34; Amos 3:2). Here unambiguously blessing: God fulfills the promise of Genesis 17:16-21 and Genesis 18:10. The covenantal attention expressed by pāqaḏ is intimate and purposeful — God did not merely allow nature to take its course; he attended to Sarah specifically.
2: לַמּוֹעֵד אֲשֶׁר-דִּבֶּר אֹתוֹ אֱלֹהִים (lammôʿēḏ ʾăšer-dibbēr ʾōtô ʾĔlōhîm) — "at the appointed time that God had spoken." Môʿēḏ (appointed time, fixed season, set meeting) — the same word used for the sacred assembly times of Israel's festival calendar (Lev 23; Num 28-29). Isaac's birth is at a divinely appointed môʿēḏ, not a random biological event — the covenant heir arrives at God's chosen moment, on God's schedule (announced in Genesis 18:10: "at the appointed time I will return to you").
3: וַיִּקְרָא אַבְרָהָם אֶת-שֶׁם-בְּנוֹ הַנּוֹלַד-לוֹ אֲשֶׁר-יָלְדָה-לּוֹ שָׂרָה יִצְחָק — "Abraham called the name of his son who was born to him, whom Sarah bore him, Isaac." Yiṣḥāq (Isaac) = "he laughs / he will laugh" — from ṣāḥaq (to laugh); God specified this name in Genesis 17:19 ("you will call his name Isaac"). The name encodes Sarah's laughter of disbelief (Genesis 18:12-13) and Abraham's laughter of joyful astonishment (Genesis 17:17) — both are transformed into the child's identity. The name is also the first Hebrew term in the Bible derived from the word for joy/laughter (ṣĕḥōq), suggesting the covenant heir's arrival is inherently a cause for rejoicing.
4: וַיָּמָל אַבְרָהָם אֶת-יִצְחָק בְּנוֹ בֶּן-שְׁמֹנַת יָמִים — "Abraham circumcised his son Isaac when he was eight days old." The circumcision is performed exactly as commanded in Genesis 17:12 ("every male among you eight days old shall be circumcised") — the narrative carefully records that the covenant sign was applied to the covenant heir on the prescribed day. כַּאֲשֶׁר צִוָּה אֹתוֹ אֱלֹהִים (as God had commanded him) — the obedience formula; the narrator marks the fulfilled command as the structural point of the verse.
5: וְאַבְרָהָם בֶּן-מְאַת שָׁנָה — "and Abraham was a hundred years old." The chronological note emphasizes the impossibility: 100 years old (cf. Genesis 17:17; Romans 4:19: "his own body was as good as dead, being about a hundred years old"). The birth of Isaac is presented as a biological impossibility overcome by divine promise — the covenant heir's origin is not from natural human capacity but from divine faithfulness to the spoken word.
6: צְחֹק עָשָׂה-לִּי אֱלֹהִים (ṣĕḥōq ʿāśāh-lî ʾĔlōhîm) — "God has made laughter for me." Ṣĕḥōq (laughter, mirth) — the same root as Isaac's name, now completed: the name was given in anticipation, and the laughter it names has arrived. כָּל-הַשֹּׁמֵעַ יִצְחַק-לִי (all who hear will laugh with me/at me) — the verb yiṣḥaq-lî is the jussive form of Isaac's name: "everyone who hears will [yiṣḥaq = Isaac]" — the impossible birth makes the name a living event, a standing proclamation of divine power. The ambiguity of "laugh with me" (joy) or "laugh at me" (astonishment) is deliberate in the Hebrew.
7: מִי מִלֵּל לְאַבְרָהָם הֵינִיקָה בָנִים שָׂרָה — "Who would have said to Abraham that Sarah would nurse children?" Millēl (Pilpel of mālal, to say, speak — an Aramaicizing form used in poetic or archaic contexts) — a rhetorical impossibility: no human being would have dared to predict it. The nursing detail (hênîqāh bānîm, nurse sons) is specific — Sarah suckles the child, proving she has milk, proving the physiological restoration of a post-menopausal woman. כִּי-יָלַדְתִּי בֵן לִזְקֻנָיו (for I have borne a son in his old age) — the son born to an old man; the miracle belongs to both parents.
8: וַיִּגְדַּל הַיֶּלֶד וַיִּגָּמַל (wayyiḡdal hayyeleḏ wayyiggāmal) — "the child grew and was weaned." Gāmal (to wean, accomplish, repay, ripen) — the weaning feast marks a significant developmental milestone; ANE practice typically involved nursing for 2-3 years. מִשְׁתֶּה גָדוֹל (great feast) — the weaning celebration is a public, community event; Isaac's growth from birth to weaning is publicly acknowledged. The feast setting introduces the conflict of Genesis 21:9-10: what Ishmael does, he does during this celebration of Isaac.
9: וַתֵּרֶא שָׂרָה אֶת-בֶּן-הָגָר הַמִּצְרִית אֲשֶׁר-יָלְדָה לְאַבְרָהָם מְצַחֵק — "Sarah saw the son of Hagar the Egyptian, whom she had borne to Abraham, mocking/laughing." Mĕṣaḥēq (Piel participle of ṣāḥaq) — the same root as Isaac's name (Yiṣḥāq). The Piel of ṣāḥaq has a stronger force than the Qal: the basic meaning is "playing, sporting, laughing," but the Piel elsewhere means "mocking" (Genesis 39:14, 17 where Potiphar's wife accuses Joseph of the same verb), "making sport" with potential sexual or aggressive overtones, or engaging in idol-worship (Genesis 32:6; Exodus 32:6). The LXX simply reads "playing" (paizonta). Paul in Galatians 4:29 uses the language of "persecution." The Hebrew text leaves the precise nature deliberately open; the narrative function is clear: Sarah perceives it as a threat to Isaac's inheritance.
10: גָּרֵשׁ הָאָמָה הַזֹּאת וְאֶת-בְּנָהּ (gārēš hāʾāmāh hazzōʾt wĕʾet-bĕnāh) — "drive out this slave woman and her son." Gāraš (to drive out, expel) — a strong term (used for the expulsion from Eden, Genesis 3:24; the driving out of nations, Exodus 34:11). Sarah's demand is harsh: ʾāmāh (slave woman, maidservant) — she does not call Hagar by name or Ishmael by name; they are reduced to "this slave woman and her son." לֹא יִירַשׁ בֶּן-הָאָמָה הַזֹּאת עִם-בְּנִי עִם-יִצְחָק — "the son of this slave woman will not inherit with my son, with Isaac." The legal concern is inheritance: Ishmael, as Abraham's firstborn, has a claim; Sarah insists on Isaac's exclusive inheritance. The double "with my son, with Isaac" is emphatic contrast.
11: וַיֵּרַע הַדָּבָר מְאֹד בְּעֵינֵי אַבְרָהָם — "the matter was very displeasing to Abraham." Rāʿaʿ (to be evil, bad, displeasing) — the idiom rāʿaʿ bĕʿênê (evil/bad in the eyes of) is the standard formula for moral/emotional displeasure. עַל אוֹדֹת בְּנוֹ (on account of his son) — it is Ishmael Abraham grieves for: his firstborn, 13 years old at Isaac's birth (Genesis 17:25), now being expelled. The narrative presents Abraham's grief as genuine parental love for Ishmael — the covenant narrowing is painful, not indifferent.
12: כִּי בְיִצְחָק יִקָּרֵא לְךָ זָרַע (kî ḇĕyiṣḥāq yiqqārēʾ lĕḵā zeraʿ) — "for through Isaac your offspring will be named/reckoned." The Niphal yiqqārēʾ (will be called, reckoned) — the covenant line passes through Isaac alone; Ishmael's exclusion is not rejection of Ishmael as a person but narrowing of the covenant inheritance. Paul cites this verse in Romans 9:7 and Hebrews 11:18 to make the theological point that physical descent alone does not define covenant membership — "not all who are descended from Israel are Israel" (Romans 9:6). The preposition bĕ (in/through Isaac) is partitive: only through this line.
13: וְגַם אֶת-בֶּן-הָאָמָה לְגוֹי אֲשִׂימֶנּוּ — "but I will also make the son of the slave woman into a nation." God's promise to Ishmael is restated: lĕgôy (into a nation) — a significant word: the same promise given to Abraham (Genesis 12:2: "I will make you a great nation") is given in a modified form to Ishmael. כִּי זַרְעֲךָ הוּא (for he is your offspring) — God's care for Ishmael is rooted in Ishmael's relationship to Abraham, not to Sarah or to the covenant per se. The principle: God's covenant does not eliminate his care for those outside the primary covenant line.
14: וַיַּשְׁכֵּם אַבְרָהָם בַּבֹּקֶר — "Abraham rose early in the morning." The same word as Genesis 22:3 (the Akedah) and Genesis 20:8 (Abimelech's early response to God's warning) — the early rising marks urgency in response to divine direction. וַיִּתֵּן אֶל-הָגָר שָׂם עַל-שִׁכְמָהּ וְאֶת-הַיֶּלֶד — "he gave to Hagar, putting [them] on her shoulder, and the child" — the Hebrew syntax is difficult: food and water are given, placed on Hagar's shoulder, along with the child (how exactly the child was carried with these provisions is unclear). Wayyĕšallĕḥehā (he sent her away) — the same verb used for Adam's expulsion from Eden (Genesis 3:23; שָׁלַח). Hagar departs for the "wilderness of Beersheba" — the dry steppe south of the settled zone.
15: וַיִּכְלוּ הַמַּיִם מִן-הַחֵמֶת — "the water in the skin was gone." Ḥēmet (skin bottle, water-skin) — water is life in the desert; the narrative precisely marks the crisis point: without water, the child will die. וַתַּשְׁלֵךְ אֶת-הַיֶּלֶד תַּחַת אַחַד הַשִּׂיחִם — "she cast the child under one of the bushes." Šālaḵ (to throw, cast) — the same verb used for casting the Egyptian children into the Nile (Exodus 1:22) — Hagar is forced to do to her son what Pharaoh would later do to Hebrew children. The irony that the Egyptian woman abandons her child in the wilderness prefigures the Egyptian king abandoning Hebrew children in the river.
16: לֹא אֶרְאֶה בְּמוֹת הַיָּלֶד — "I cannot watch the boy die." Hagar moves away "a bowshot away" — the Hebrew bow-distance (kĕmĕṭaḥăwê qešet) is a precise practical measure: far enough not to see but close enough to hear. Wattiśśāʾ ʾet-qōlāh watēḇk (she lifted up her voice and wept) — the mourning formula; Hagar's weeping mirrors her weeping in Genesis 16. The mother who received the divine promise of Ishmael's greatness (Genesis 16:10-12) is now at the point of watching him die — the gap between promise and visible reality is absolute.
17: וַיִּשְׁמַע אֱלֹהִים אֶת-קוֹל הַנַּעַר (wayyišmaʿ ʾĔlōhîm ʾet-qôl hannāʿar) — "God heard the voice of the boy." The boy's name, Ishmael ("God hears"), is enacted: God hears (šāmaʿ) the one whose name means "God hears." The divine hearing is not Hagar's weeping but the boy's crying — the covenant of the name (Genesis 16:11: "for the LORD has heard your affliction") is fulfilled in its next generation. מַלְאַךְ אֱלֹהִים (angel of God) — the second appearance of the divine messenger to Hagar (cf. Genesis 16:7); qārāʾ from heaven (called from heaven) — the heavenly voice rather than the visible manifestation of Genesis 16:7.
18: קוּמִי שְׂאִי אֶת-הַנַּעַר (qûmî śĕʾî ʾet-hannāʿar) — "rise, lift up the boy." The double imperative (qûmî, rise; śĕʾî, lift up) is urgent. כִּי-לְגוֹי גָּדוֹל אֲשִׂימֶנּוּ — "for I will make him into a great nation." The promise is the same as Genesis 17:20 (God's covenant with Ishmael: "I will make him a great nation") and echoes the Abrahamic promise of Genesis 12:2. The angel's words repeat the content of the name: hear it again, Hagar — what God said, God will do.
19: וַיִּפְקַח אֱלֹהִים אֶת-עֵינֶיהָ וַתֵּרֶא בְּאֵר מָיִם — "God opened her eyes and she saw a well of water." Pāqaḥ (to open, uncover — eyes) — the same verb as in Genesis 3:5, 7 where the serpent promises "your eyes will be opened" after eating the forbidden fruit, and in 3:7 where "the eyes of both of them were opened." The ironic reversal is clear: in Eden, eyes opened to shame and knowledge of evil; here, eyes are opened to water and life. The divine eye-opening is mercy, not judgment. The well was presumably there all along — Hagar could not see it until God opened her eyes.
20: וַיְהִי אֱלֹהִים אֶת-הַנַּעַר (wayhî ʾĔlōhîm ʾet-hannāʿar) — "God was with the boy." The summary formula YHWH/ʾĔlōhîm was with X is the highest divine-favor statement in the OT narrative (with Joseph, Genesis 39:2, 21, 23; with David, 1 Samuel 16:18; Genesis 18:12, 14; with Jesus, Acts 10:38). Ishmael grows up with the divine presence — he is not abandoned. וַיְהִי רֹבֶה קַשָּׁת (he became an archer) — rōbeh qaššāt (bow-shooter, archer) — fulfilling the wild-donkey-man oracle of Genesis 16:12: independent, free, skilled in desert survival.
21: וַתִּקַּח-לוֹ אִמּוֹ אִשָּׁה מֵאֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם — "his mother took a wife for him from the land of Egypt." Hagar the Egyptian secures an Egyptian wife for Ishmael — he becomes the ancestor of peoples linked to Egypt, consistent with the Arabian/Ishmaelite peoples of the OT's geographic horizon. The mother arranges the marriage (the father's role is normally fulfilled here by the mother because Abraham has expelled them) — Hagar exercises the paternal prerogative of marriage arrangement for her son.
22: אֱלֹהִים עִמְּךָ בְּכֹל אֲשֶׁר-אַתָּה עֹשֶׂה — "God is with you in all that you do." Abimelech and Phicol recognize the divine presence with Abraham — the same recognition formula that Laban will use (Genesis 31:5: "God of your father is with you") and that will be repeated for Isaac (Genesis 26:28) and Joseph (Genesis 39:2-3). Pîḵōl (Phicol) — the commander of Abimelech's forces; his inclusion in the treaty-request elevates it to a state treaty, not merely a personal agreement.
23: אִם-תִּשְׁקֹר לִי וּלְנִינִי וּלְנֶכְדִּי — "that you will not deal falsely with me, or with my son's son, or with my grandson." Šāqar (to deal falsely, betray — specifically covenantal betrayal, false-dealing within a sworn relationship; cf. Psalms 44:17; Genesis 89:33). Nîn wāneḵeḏ (offspring and descendant) — a rare pair of terms for extended lineage, appearing also in Job 18:19 and Isaiah 14:22. Abimelech is asking for a multi-generational treaty — the covenant of kindness he showed Abraham should bind their descendants. כַּחֶסֶד אֲשֶׁר-עָשִׂיתִי עִמְּךָ (kaḥeseḏ ʾăšer-ʿāśîtî ʿimmĕḵā) — "as the kindness I have done with you" — ḥeseḏ (covenant-loyalty, steadfast love) is the word for Abimelech's conduct: he treated Abraham with ḥeseḏ even after being deceived.
24: אָנֹכִי אִשָּׁבֵעַ (ʾānōḵî ʾiššāḇēaʿ, I will swear) — Abraham accepts the treaty request with the emphatic first-person pronoun ʾānōḵî (I, myself) + the Niphal of šāḇaʿ (to swear, bind oneself by oath). The swearing establishes a formal covenant relationship between Abraham and the Philistine king — a non-Israelite political treaty rooted in recognized divine presence.
25: וְהוֹכִחַ אַבְרָהָם אֶת-אֲבִימֶלֶךְ (wĕhôkîaḥ ʾaḇrāhām ʾet-ʾăḇîmeleḵ) — "Abraham complained/argued with Abimelech." Yākaḥ (Hiphil, to reprove, argue, bring a legal complaint against) — a formal disputational term; Abraham raises a legal grievance. עַל-אֹדוֹת בְּאֵר הַמַּיִם (about a well of water) — water rights in the Negev were a matter of survival; disputes over wells are a recurring motif in the patriarchal narratives (Genesis 26:15-22; Exodus 2:17). Abraham complains that Abimelech's servants seized the well he dug.
26: Abimelech's denial: "I do not know who did this thing / you did not tell me / I heard about it only today" — three successive disclaimers creating a formal forensic defense. The three-part denial is a rhetorical unit that completely absolves Abimelech (by his own account) of responsibility for the well-seizure. The narrative does not adjudicate between the claims; it proceeds to the treaty that resolves the dispute.
27: וַיִּכְרְתוּ שְׁנֵיהֶם בְּרִית (wayyiḵrĕtû šĕnêhem bĕrît) — "the two of them cut a covenant." Kārat bĕrît (to cut a covenant) — the standard treaty formula (cf. 15:18 for the Abrahamic covenant, 31:44 for Jacob and Laban). The sheep and cattle Abraham gave (Genesis 21:27) are both the gift of friendship and possibly the sacrificial animals for the covenant ceremony (parties to a covenant sometimes offered sacrifices at the ratification).
28: וַיַּצֵּב אַבְרָהָם אֶת-שֶׁבַע כִּבְשֹׂת הַצֹּאן לְבַדְּהֶן — "Abraham set apart seven ewe lambs by themselves." Šeḇaʿ kĕḇāśôt (seven ewe lambs) — the word šeḇaʿ (seven) is the root of the Beersheba name: bĕʾēr šeḇaʿ = "well of seven" or "well of the oath" (šĕḇuʿāh = oath, from the same root šāḇaʿ). The seven lambs function as an oath-token — a material confirmation of the sworn covenant.
29: Abimelech's question about the seven lambs creates the legal dialogue through which Abraham explains the symbolic significance — a teaching moment embedded in the covenant ceremony. The question-and-answer pattern (Abimelech asks → Abraham explains) is the standard didactic structure for covenant ceremonies and legal transactions in the OT, where formal questioning ensures that all parties understand the legal significance of the symbolic act.
30: כִּי אֶת-שֶׁבַע כְּבָשֹׂת תִּקַּח מִיָּדִי בַּעֲבוּר תִּהְיֶה-לִּי לְעֵדָה אֲשֶׁר חָפַרְתִּי אֶת-הַבְּאֵר הַזֹּאת — "the seven lambs you shall accept from my hand so that it may be a witness for me that I dug this well." ʿEḏāh (witness, testimony) — the seven lambs serve as a legal witness object: the acceptance of the lambs by Abimelech constitutes acknowledgment that Abraham dug the well and owns the water rights. Legal documentation in the ANE used symbolic objects as substitute for written contracts.
31: עַל-כֵּן קָרָא לַמָּקוֹם הַהוּא בְּאֵר שָׁבַע (ʿal-kēn qārāʾ lammāqôm hahûʾ bĕʾēr šāḇaʿ) — "therefore that place was called Beersheba." The etymology is double: bĕʾēr šāḇaʿ = (1) Well of Seven (šeḇaʿ = seven — the seven lambs); or (2) Well of the Oath (šĕḇuʿāh = oath, from šāḇaʿ = to swear). Both meanings are activated simultaneously — the place name encodes the dual significance of the number seven and the covenant oath. Beersheba will become one of the key patriarchal sites (Genesis 26:23-25, 33; Genesis 28:10; Genesis 46:1) and eventually the southern boundary marker of Israel ("from Dan to Beersheba").
32: אֶרֶץ פְּלִשְׁתִּים (ʾereṣ pĕlištîm, land of the Philistines) — the anachronistic reference to "the land of the Philistines" is noted by scholars: the Philistines as a settled people in Canaan's coastal plain are historically attested from the 12th century BCE (Sea Peoples), later than the patriarchal period. This may reflect: (1) an updating gloss by a later editor; (2) the use of an ancient ethnic name for peoples of that region before the canonical Philistine settlement; (3) a literary identification with the later Philistine territory.
33: וַיִּטַּע אֶשֶׁל בִּבְאֵר שָׁבַע וַיִּקְרָא-שָׁם בְּשֵׁם יְהוָה אֵל עוֹלָם — "Abraham planted a tamarisk tree at Beersheba and called there on the name of the LORD, the Eternal God." ʾĒšel (tamarisk) — a tree that provides shade in the harsh Negev; associated with sanctuary or memorial plantings (Saul also plants one in 1 Samuel 31:13). ʾĒl ʿôlām (the Eternal God/God of eternity) — Abraham's divine name at Beersheba; ʿôlām is here used as a divine epithet ("the Eternal One") rather than modifying the covenant (cf. Genesis 17:7, 13, 19 where ʿôlām modifies the covenant itself). The attribute of God's eternal nature is new in the Genesis divine-name vocabulary.
34: וַיָּגָר אַבְרָהָם בְּאֶרֶץ פְּלִשְׁתִּים יָמִים רַבִּים — "Abraham sojourned in the land of the Philistines many days." Yāmîm rabbîm (many days) — an indefinite long period; the same phrase used for the sojourning of Jacob (Genesis 37:34) and others. The extended Beersheba period forms the interval before the Akedah of Genesis 22 — the narrative pause before the climactic test. Abraham the sojourner continues his pattern: possessing nothing permanently in the promised land, living as a guest among its inhabitants.
